title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
summary: The government impounded the petitioner's passport without giving reasons. The Court expanded the scope of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) to include the right to go abroad. It held that 'procedure established by law' must be right, just, and fair — not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. The case linked Articles 14, 19, and 21 as a 'golden triangle' of rights.
key_holdings: Right to travel abroad is part of 'personal liberty' under Article 21
'Procedure established by law' must satisfy standards of natural justice
Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive — they form an integrated charter of rights
Any law affecting personal liberty must be just, fair, and reasonable
key_quotes: The law must be right, just and fair and not arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive.
Basic rights cluster around man in his individual capacity and in his social life.
why_important: Landmark expansion of Article 21 beyond mere physical liberty. Established that fundamental rights are interlinked and that state action must satisfy substantive due process.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Establishes procedural fairness standards relevant to tax enforcement and financial penalty proceedings.
